FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes prospective data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=10,246, recruited from 21 sites around the U.S., baseline ages 9-10, ABCD 4.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multiple logistic regression on prospective ABCD cohort data (baseline screen time predicting year-two eating disorder symptoms; cross-sectional analyses at year two).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Eating and Weight Disorders (2024); no indication of duplicate or preprint of already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports OR for social media use (hours/day) predicting fear of obesity (OR 1.55, 95% CI 1.21-1.98, p=0.001), self-worth tied to weight (OR 1.30), compensatory behaviors (OR 1.18), binge eating (OR 1.28), distress with binge eating (OR 1.31) at two-year follow-up. Table 3 reports SMAQ (problematic social media use) predicting eating disorder symptoms (OR 1.38-1.79) and MPIQ (problematic mobile phone use) predicting eating disorder symptoms (OR 1.26-1.82). Social media hours and SMAQ/MPIQ are phone/SM-specific variables; eating disorder symptoms (fear of weight gain, binge eating, compensatory behaviors) are individual-trait health outcomes on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression OR with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Tables 2 and 3 for SM/phone variables predicting eating disorder symptoms.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout Tables 2 and 3 (ORs ranging from 1.05 to 1.82 with CIs and p-values).

FT_EC7_reason: EC7 does not apply: both longitudinal analyses (baseline SM as IV predicting year-two eating disorder symptoms) and cross-sectional analyses (SM as IV predicting year-two eating disorder symptoms) are present, with SM serving as IV throughout. SM is IV, not DV.
notes: Paper reports longitudinal associations of total screen time and social media use as exposures (IV) predicting eating disorder symptoms (DV), plus cross-sectional analyses of SMAQ and MPIQ as IVs. Clear INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the ABCD Study (ABCD Annual Curated Release 5.0), longitudinal study of brain development in the United States, n=8,489, baseline ages 9-10, 21 study sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multinomial logistic regression and linear regression on ABCD data to examine associations between pet ownership type and physical activity, sports involvement, and screen time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Anthrozoos (2024); no indication of duplicate or preprint of already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The screen time variables used are: (1) Parent Screen Time (PSQ) described as 'a parent self-report of screen time behaviors and behavior modeling' measuring overall screen time in minutes per week, and (2) Youth Screen Time (STQ) described as 'a youth self-report measure which includes customized questions about the overall amount of time that the person spends using visual media on a typical weekday and weekend day.' Both are generic visual media composites. Step 2 composite check: the STQ and PSQ are multi-device, multi-content aggregates of all visual media use (not exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items). No per-modality breakout for social media or phone specifically appears in any results table. The IV in all analyses is pet ownership type (active, passive, no pet); screen time serves as DV. The screen time DV does not qualify as a phone/SM variable under Step 2 because it includes TV and other non-phone/non-SM visual media without separate breakout.

notes: Screen time measures (PSQ, STQ) are generic visual media composites including TV; no phone/SM-specific variable or estimate appears anywhere in the paper. Pet ownership is the IV. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Participants comprised 11,876 adolescents assessed annually from ages 9-12 years as part of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (ABCD), data from the US National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) Data Archive (NDA).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal network analysis using g-LASSO models on ABCD data across three time points to examine associations between screen usage types and psychotic-like experiences (PLEs).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Comprehensive Psychiatry (2024); no indication of duplicate or preprint of already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 2-4 report network edge weights at each time point for individually-measured phone/SM-specific variables: ScrSM (screen time on social media), ScrText (screen time texting), and ScrVChat (screen time on video chats) paired with PLE symptoms (e.g., ScrText-ScrSM edge weight 0.312 at T1; ScrSM-ScrVChat edge weight 0.190 at T1; ScrText node has the highest centrality across all time points). These three variables form the 'digital media for socializing' cluster paired with PLE nodes (auditory hallucinations, visual hallucinations, difficulty concentrating, etc.) which are individual-trait psychiatric symptoms. Social media, texting, and video chat each have their own distinct network edge weights connecting to PLEs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network edge weights (numeric estimates) reported in Tables 2, 3, and 4 for all node pairs across three time points; centrality scores (expected influence, betweenness, closeness) reported in Figures 4-9.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative network edge weights and centrality indices reported for all three time points in Tables 2-4 and Figures 4-9.

FT_EC7_reason: EC7 does not apply: the network analysis is an undirected longitudinal network model where edges between SM/phone nodes (ScrSM, ScrText, ScrVChat) and PLE nodes are bidirectional by design (network edges do not have a direction; both SM and PLEs are simultaneously nodes in the network). SM is not exclusively a DV; the network edges represent symmetric associations. Furthermore, the cross-sectional network structure is estimated at each time point.
notes: Longitudinal network analysis with social media, texting, and video chat as distinct nodes alongside PLE symptom nodes. The g-LASSO network approach produces undirected edges, so SM cannot be characterized as DV-only. Clear INCLUDE.
